name: Smoking Salmon and Trout Part II - Filleting and Boning
recipeIngredient: A very sharp knife (and a whet stone and a sharpening steel nearby) is essential.
recipeInstructions:
1. Skin on Fillets:
- Begin at the vent (anus), making a cut on either side of the anal fin just deep enough to reach the backbone. Make these cuts all the way back to the tail.
- Start the next cut where the head has been removed, on top of the backbone. Cut through the fish from back to belly, lengthways right down to the tail. You will encounter belly bones that become tougher as the fish gets larger. The sharp knife is crucial here; it must be able to easily cut through the belly bones.
- As you continue cutting from head to tail along the backbone, work the knife with its cutting edge slightly slanted towards the bone.
- Remove the first fillet and turn the fish over to cut the second fillet. There should be very little meat left on the backbone.
- Now remove the belly bones without losing any meat. This leaves a boneless fillet except for a line of long, thin bones just above where the backbone used to be. Feel these sharp ends with your fingertip.
- These last bones can be removed in a strip by making a cut on either side of the row, just to the skin. This final deboning will somewhat affect the appearance and utility of smoked products when thinly sliced. A more meticulous method is to remove these bones one at a time using small needle-nose pliers. If the bones don't pull out easily, do it after smoking.
2. Easy Skinless Fillets:
- Start with whole fish, uncleaned and head on.
- Make the first cut just below the gills done to the backbone at a slight angle. Turn the knife and cut along the backbone to the tail at a slight angle so as to "float" over the belly bones. Stop just short of the tail and peel back the skin-on fillet without tearing it away from where it is attached to the fish at the tail.
- Lay the fillet on the table, skin side down, and start a cut at the tail. Cut down to but not through the skin and turn the blade sideways. Cut the fillet away from the skin and continue back up the fillet to the other end.
- Turn over the fish and repeat. Be careful not to puncture the intestines, bladder, or stomach. You should now have two skinless fillets with the guts still attached to the carcass. This method avoids scaling or cleaning but results in minimal meat and belly skin loss. It works best on larger fish (4 lbs and up).
3. Defatting Fat Fish:
- Salmon and trout are fat fish, and you may want to defat them for various reasons, such as adhering to a low-fat diet or removing contaminants concentrated in the fat tissues from certain waters.
- When filleting, leave plenty of meat on the backbone where the meat is especially fatty. Cut off the belly portion. Skin the fillet, leaving about 1/8 inch (0.25 cm) of meat on the skin.
- Make cuts on either side of the lateral line, lift it out and discard it.
4. Boning Small Trout:
- French Presentation I - Through the back with the belly uncut:
Snip the fins off with scissors.
With a small knife, cut the gills loose at the throat. Use your fingers to pull the gills loose along with most of the intestines.
Make a cut along one side of the backbone, working the meat loose from the bone and all the way to the belly without cutting the belly skin.
Repeat on the other side. With scissors, snip the backbone as close to the tail as possible and again at the head.
Finish cleaning the belly cavity with your fingers. The fish is now ready for smoking or cooking with its head and tail intact.
- French Presentation II - Boning through the belly:
After cleaning, start by making a cut from the vent along each side of the backbone to the tail.
Work the meat loose from the rib cage and along the backbone to but not through the skin of the back.
Snip off the backbone close to the tail. Pull the bone free up to the head and snip it off.
Scissor off the fins. The fish is now ready for smoking or cooking.
